text: Thank you very much. I'm Valhag Nicolian presenting on behalf of the Oregon Health and Science University Hernia Center. This is a presentation that will be presented by Samuel Torres-Landa, one of our residents, and performed by Dr. Sean B. Orenstein. We'll be discussing a robotic transabdominal preperitoneal repair of a large morgagni hernia.
Dr. Orenstein and Dr. Napoleon serve as consultants and instructors for multiple groups, though these are not relevant to today's presentation.

It's a forty-nine-year-old woman who presented with symptoms of epigastric and retrosternal discomfort. Hernia risk factors and medical history were reviewed. She had well-controlled diabetes, was a nonsmoker, had a BMI of thirty-three, and well-controlled asthma. Her surgical history was notable for a laparoscopic cholecystectomy, a laparoscopic rune-wide gastric bypass, and an open left inguinal hernia. On exam, she had no obvious hernias and had well-healed trocar sites.

She presented to our clinic with cross-sectional imaging already obtained, which identified a large right congenital diaphragmatic defect containing colon, small intestine, and omentum. We can see the defect there. The defect measured roughly five to six centimeters in largest diameter. Given these findings, we discussed morganic hernias with her, highlighted that they're congenital in nature, usually located on the right side, also known as retrosternal or parasternal hernias.

Invasive approaches are well described and provide significant benefits for patients with a low burden of recurrence and a shorter convalescence. We plan to move forward with a robotic repair. For these operations, patients are positioned with their arms out with ten to fifteen degrees of reverse Trendelenburg. We essentially do a lower dock as you would for an epigastric hernia repair.

The surgical team utilizes instruments including tip-up forceps, bipolar forceps, and monopolar scissors to perform a suture cut. Lightweight uncoated polypropylene mesh is prepared for use in the preperitoneal repair of the hernia.

The surgeon assesses the hernia, revealing a large volume of small intestine and colon within the hernia sac. The bowel is easily reduced without significant adhesions, and no enterotomies are made. The remaining omentum is reduced, clearly exposing the margins of the Morgagni defect.

The surgeon assesses the interior of the hernia sac while the patient is on dual lung ventilation, keeping the right lung down for easier dissection. A peritoneal flap is developed approximately ten centimeters below the lower margins of the Morgagni hernia, carefully staying within the areolar tissue plane to avoid injury to the overlying fascia.

The surgeon carefully dissects cephalad along the linea alba, preserving the anterior fascia while identifying the hernia margin. The diastasis measuring approximately three to four centimeters is visible.

The surgeon is positioned at the hernia margin, reducing the hernia sac while working on the left side. Visible transversus abdominis muscle fibers are observed during the procedure. The areolar tissue is also visible in the surgical field.

The surgeon reduces the hernia sac, noting the ease of reduction. During the process, a hole is inadvertently created in the pleura. The lung remains deflated due to dual lung ventilation, and the hernia sac is assessed for fenestrations, with none observed.
The surgeon completes the reduction of the hernia sac and proceeds with circumferential dissection around the hernia defect, preparing the area for broad mesh coverage.

The surgeon begins on the right side, identifying and exposing the diaphragm muscle fibers while reducing the peritoneum. The procedure is straightforward due to the absence of previous operations in this area. On the left side, the central tendon of the diaphragm is clearly visualized, and efforts are made to develop a broad space for mesh coverage.

The surgeon completes the dissection and prepares for defect closure, using a permanent barbed suture to re-approximate the hernia in a transverse orientation. The tissue comes together smoothly without the need for Pledgets.
The surgeon completes the defect closure without tension, preparing the site for mesh placement.

The surgeon places a lightweight polypropylene macroporous mesh over the hernia defect, securing it with multiple interrupted absorbable sutures (polysorb). The mesh provides broad coverage of the defect, with no additional fixation methods used.
The surgeon performs a standard flap closure using an absorbable barbed suture, bringing the flap together smoothly without tension. The redundant hernia sac is visible but not ligated.

And that's a closure. The patient did wonderful. Postoperatively, we obtained the chest x-ray, which showed a cap in the thorax, but no respiratory issues. A chest tube was not required. Patient was discharged on postoperative day two and is doing well in the post-op surveillance period. Diaphragmatic hernias should be repaired to reduce the risk of strangulation. A preparatory repair can allow for broad mass coverage. Minimally invasive repairs associated with low burden of occurrence and lower morbidity than open repairs.
Thank you very much and we'd be happy to address any questions.
